input.industry: Multi-asset fintech. CFD and futures trading platform.
input.geography: The group holds licences in the UK, Australia, Cyprus, Israel and New Zealand. It added UAE and Canadian licences during 2025, secured authorisation for a representative office in Colombia, and completed the acquisition of Mehta Equities in India in February 2026.

company_profile.industry: Multi-asset fintech. CFD and futures trading platform.
company_profile.geography: The group holds licences in the UK, Australia, Cyprus, Israel and New Zealand. It added UAE and Canadian licences during 2025, secured authorisation for a representative office in Colombia, and completed the acquisition of Mehta Equities in India in February 2026.
company_profile.employee_count: GAP — not disclosed in source material
company_profile.description: Plus500 runs proprietary trading platforms across multiple asset classes. It listed in London in July 2013 and has been the best-performing stock on the FTSE All-Share Index on a total return basis from IPO to the end of 2025. The group holds licences in the UK, Australia, Cyprus, Israel and New Zealand. It added UAE and Canadian licences during 2025, secured authorisation for a representative office in Colombia, and completed the acquisition of Mehta Equities in India in February 2026. Customers trade OTC products, primarily CFDs, alongside share dealing and US futures and options on futures. Efficiency defines this business. A lean organisation running a mobile-first proprietary stack produces a 44% EBITDA margin and carries no debt. Evidence note: Financials, KPIs and regulatory facts come from Plus500's public LSE filings and FY2025 results. Market-sizing figures are analyst-constructed estimates with stated assumptions, not third-party market data. A live engagement would triangulate these against named paid sources. Evidence grade: High. The target is LSE-listed and files under UK disclosure rules. LSE interim reporting runs less granular than US quarterly filings, so intra-year detail is thinner than for a Nasdaq-listed comparable.

key_facts.label: Ownership
key_facts.value: Public. LSE: PLUS since 24 July 2013. Constituent of the FTSE 250 and STOXX Europe 600.

business_model.revenue_model: OTC, around 87% of revenue. Spreads and financing on CFDs across shares, forex, commodities, indices and crypto. Mobile users generate 89% of it. Around half now comes from customers of more than five years' standing, double the share of three years ago. Non-OTC, around 12.6% of revenue. Mostly the US futures business, which passed $100 million in revenue for the first time in FY2025. It covers B2C through Plus500 Futures, B2B institutional clearing and technology, and now prediction markets. CME Group appointed Plus500 as clearing partner for its event-based contracts platform launched with FanDuel. Topstep signed Plus500 as its exclusive clearing and technology provider. In February 2026 Plus500 launched event-based contracts through Kalshi, the first CFTC-regulated prediction-markets exchange, clearing the trades itself. Interest income on customer balances contributed $29.6 million in H1 2025. Capital allocation: Plus500 returned $380 million to shareholders in 2025 and has repurchased about 38% of its shares over time. It announced a further $187.5 million package combining a $100 million buyback with $87.5 million in dividends.
business_model.value_proposition: Fast, reliable, mobile-first access to leveraged trading across thousands of instruments, on a platform Plus500 built and owns end to end. The pitch is execution quality and simplicity, not the lowest price.
key_activities: Building and running the proprietary platform.
Acquiring customers through the in-house marketing engine under strict return-on-investment rules.
Securing and maintaining licences across jurisdictions.
Operating clearing infrastructure for the futures and prediction-markets business.
Buying companies that add capability or market access, as with Mehta Equities.
key_resources: The proprietary technology stack, which drives both the product and the marketing engine.
Regulatory licences across the UK, Australia, Cyprus, Israel, New Zealand, UAE and Canada.
Clearing memberships with Kalshi Klear and ICE Clear US.
About $0.8 billion in cash and no debt.
Marketing technology built on big data and AI that cut acquisition cost more than 10% in a year.
channels: Direct to consumer through mobile, which generates 89% of OTC revenue.
Digital acquisition run on proprietary marketing technology.
B2B institutional partnerships with CME and Topstep, a channel that did not exist two years ago.
business_model.customer_relationships: Self-service and low-touch by design, which is what keeps the cost base lean. Plus500 has shifted toward longevity: around half of FY2025 OTC revenue came from customers who have traded with it for more than five years, double the share three years earlier. Institutional relationships work the other way, running on long contracts with high switching costs.
cost_structure: Customer acquisition and marketing dominate, with average user acquisition cost of $1,237 in H1 2025, down from $1,489.
Staff costs stay low relative to peers, which is where the margin comes from.

market_overview.narrative: Plus500 competes in retail CFD and OTC derivatives, and increasingly in US listed futures and prediction markets. The CFD market is mature and heavily regulated. ESMA leverage caps have capped the European opportunity, which pushed every incumbent to diversify by geography and product. Plus500 is executing that playbook faster than most. The move that matters is its migration from CFD broker into regulated US market infrastructure: clearing, futures and prediction markets. That takes Plus500 up the value chain into a segment with sturdier economics and less regulatory fragility. Prediction markets are the frontier. The segment is young, CFTC-regulated and growing fast, and Plus500 took an infrastructure position through CME, FanDuel, Kalshi and Topstep rather than competing as another retail venue.
market_overview.market_size_summary: No independent third-party market-size figure is cited in the source material. See Market Sizing (TAM/SAM/SOM) for the analyst-constructed estimate, built from Plus500's disclosed economics and stated penetration assumptions rather than third-party market data.

key_stats.label: Non-OTC revenue
key_stats.value: Above $100M, around 12.6% of group, up from around 10% in FY2024

key_stats.label: Average deposit per active user
key_stats.value: About $26,900, more than double the prior year

key_stats.label: Mobile and tablet share of OTC revenue

market_overview.regional_notes: The group holds licences in the UK, Australia, Cyprus, Israel and New Zealand. It added UAE and Canadian licences during 2025, secured authorisation for a representative office in Colombia, and completed the acquisition of Mehta Equities in India in February 2026.

tam.value: Low tens of billions of dollars a year (analyst-constructed estimate)
tam.description: Global retail OTC and CFD trading, plus US retail futures and prediction markets, generates a revenue pool in the low tens of billions of dollars a year. The US futures and prediction-markets segment is the fastest-growing and least penetrated part of it.

sam.value: Low single-digit billions a year (analyst-constructed estimate)
sam.description: Retail derivatives revenue in the jurisdictions where Plus500 holds licences, now including India through Mehta, runs to an estimated low single-digit billions a year.

som.description: Plus500 captures $792.4 million (FY2025), and it does so with 242,440 active customers at $3,268 ARPU. Revenue growth is coming from customer value rather than customer count. The runway sits in the non-OTC engine, in new geographies (India, Latin America, the Gulf) and in prediction markets.

tam_sam_som.methodology_note: Analyst-constructed estimate. We built this from Plus500's disclosed economics and stated penetration assumptions rather than third-party market data. Treat it as directional. A live engagement would triangulate against named paid sources. Sizing is not the issue. The question worth diligence is whether the non-OTC engine grows fast enough to outrun a shrinking OTC customer base.

industry_trends.title: Regulation has capped retail CFDs
industry_trends.description: ESMA and FCA leverage caps permanently compressed the European CFD opportunity. This is the force driving Plus500's diversification, and it explains why its OTC customer count keeps sliding.

industry_trends.title: Regulated prediction markets are emerging
industry_trends.description: A new, fast-growing, CFTC-regulated US segment. Plus500 moved early and positioned itself as infrastructure through clearing rather than as another venue, which is the more defensible role if the segment scales.

industry_trends.title: Brokers are monetising their technology as B2B infrastructure
industry_trends.description: Firms with superior stacks now sell clearing and technology to institutions, as Plus500 does for CME and Topstep. The revenue is stickier and better quality than retail acquisition.

industry_trends.title: The sector is chasing higher-value customers
industry_trends.description: Rising acquisition costs pushed brokers from volume toward premium and longevity models. Plus500 leads on this: fewer customers, higher ARPU, longer tenure.

competitors.positioning: GAP — not disclosed in source material
strengths: The largest UK-listed CFD and spread-betting incumbent
Scale
Brand heritage
A broad product set and real institutional presence
weaknesses: A higher cost base and materially lower operating margins than Plus500
competitors.estimated_market_share: GAP — not disclosed in source material

competitors.positioning: GAP — not disclosed in source material

Strong institutional and B2B partnerships
weaknesses: Greater earnings sensitivity to market conditions

competitors.positioning: GAP — not disclosed in source material

Roughly 40 million registered users
Strong crypto and brand presence
weaknesses: Far weaker margin quality, with heavy reliance on thin-margin crypto intermediation

strengths: Zero-commission pricing winning users fast across Plus500's core European markets

competitors.positioning: GAP — not disclosed in source material

Widening products
Competitive pricing
Fast European growth
competitors.estimated_market_share: GAP — not disclosed in source material

competitors.positioning: GAP — not disclosed in source material

weaknesses: Weaker in the mobile-first mass-market CFD segment
competitors.estimated_market_share: GAP — not disclosed in source material

competitor_landscape.competitive_intensity_narrative: Plus500 does not differentiate on product. It differentiates on operating efficiency and technology. Its 44% EBITDA margin and lean cost base sit structurally above IG and CMC, and its capital returns lead the sector. The open question is whether efficiency alone holds off commission-free challengers eroding the retail base from below while Interactive Brokers contests the futures upside from above.

customer_segments.name: Premium long-tenure OTC traders (the core, and the highest value)
customer_segments.description: Experienced traders who have stayed with Plus500 for years and now generate around half of OTC revenue. The strategic focus of the whole business.
customer_segments.estimated_size: GAP — not disclosed in source material
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Large deposits averaging around $26,900, mobile-first (89% of OTC revenue), long retention, and less price sensitivity than newer users.

customer_segments.name: Mainstream retail CFD traders (volume, declining)
customer_segments.description: The traditional base that built the company and is now shrinking, with active customers down 5% and new customers down 11%.
customer_segments.estimated_size: GAP — not disclosed in source material
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Price-sensitive, promotion-driven, quick to churn. This segment takes the full force of commission-free competitors and regulatory leverage caps.

customer_segments.name: US futures retail traders (growth)
customer_segments.description: Traders using Plus500 Futures for regulated US access. They made up around 17% of new customers in H1 2025.
customer_segments.buying_behavior: More sophisticated and more engaged than the CFD base.

customer_segments.name: B2B and institutional clients (emerging, strategic)
customer_segments.description: CME, Topstep and clearing counterparties buying Plus500's infrastructure rather than its retail product.
customer_segments.estimated_size: GAP — not disclosed in source material
customer_segments.buying_behavior: Long procurement cycles, very high switching costs, multi-year contracts. This is the stickiest revenue Plus500 has.

strengths: The best profitability in the sector: a 44% EBITDA margin, no debt, about $0.8B in cash.
A proprietary stack driving both product and marketing, which cut acquisition cost more than 10% in a year.
Capital discipline few listed peers match: about 38% of shares repurchased, $380M returned in 2025.
A first-mover position in regulated US prediction-markets infrastructure.
Improving revenue quality: ARPU up 8% to a record $3,268, with half of OTC revenue from customers of five years or more.
weaknesses: The customer base is shrinking. Active customers fell 5% and new customers 11%.
Around 87% of revenue still comes from OTC and CFDs, a product class regulators keep targeting.
Growth comes from ARPU rather than volume, and ARPU has a ceiling.
Plus500 is smaller than IG or Interactive Brokers in the futures arena it is entering.
opportunities: Scaling the non-OTC and US futures engine past $100M, the clearest growth path available.
Prediction markets through Kalshi and CME/FanDuel: early, potentially large, defensible as infrastructure.
B2B clearing and technology as high-margin, sticky, long-contract revenue.
Geographic expansion into India through Mehta, Latin America through Colombia, and the UAE and Canada.
threats: Further regulatory tightening on CFDs in core European markets.
Trading 212 and XTB eroding the retail base on price.
Interactive Brokers and incumbents contesting the US futures and clearing upside.
Regulatory uncertainty in prediction markets, where the growth thesis rests on a young and politically contested regime.

forces.rationale: Listed CFD incumbents, commission-free challengers and multi-asset platforms all chase the same retail pool.
Multi-jurisdiction licences, clearing memberships and a proprietary stack form a real barrier. The clearing relationships with Kalshi Klear and ICE Clear US are especially hard to copy.
Retail users switch easily and compare prices. Plus500's answer has been to build long-tenure, high-deposit customers who behave far more stickily than the segment average.
Owning the platform rather than licensing one cuts supplier dependence sharply, a structural advantage over brokers running vendor technology.
Commission-free equities apps, direct futures brokers and crypto exchanges each replace part of the CFD proposition, especially for newer and lower-value users.

porters_five_forces.overall_attractiveness: Plus500 defends its position with cost and technology rather than product uniqueness. That holds well against incumbents and less well against price-led challengers at the entry level.

commercial_risks.title: Regulatory action on CFDs
commercial_risks.description: Around 87% of revenue comes from a product class UK and EU regulators keep in their sights.
commercial_risks.mitigation: The diversification into US futures, clearing, prediction markets and new geographies is exactly this hedge.

commercial_risks.title: A contracting customer base
commercial_risks.description: Active customers fell 5% and new customers 11%, and only rising ARPU kept revenue growing. Management chose value over volume and executed it well. ARPU still has a ceiling, and volume decline may eventually outrun value uplift.
commercial_risks.mitigation: The premium strategy is deliberate, and it needs the non-OTC engine to scale before the ceiling arrives.

commercial_risks.title: Prediction-markets regulatory uncertainty
commercial_risks.description: A key growth pillar rests on a nascent CFTC regime and on partner platforms including Kalshi and CME/FanDuel.
commercial_risks.mitigation: Plus500 positioned itself as clearing infrastructure rather than as a directional venue operator, which is the more defensible role.

commercial_risks.title: Concentration in a maturing core
commercial_risks.description: The OTC engine is mature and structurally capped, so non-OTC must scale enough to offset it.
commercial_risks.mitigation: Non-OTC grew from around 10% to around 12.6% of revenue in one year, which points the right way from a small base.

commercial_risks.title: Israel operations
commercial_risks.description: Core operations and regulatory domicile include Israel.

commercial_risks.title: Execution risk on new markets
commercial_risks.description: India, Latin America, the UAE and Canada are all unproven for Plus500.
commercial_risks.mitigation: The acquisitions are small, capability-led and disciplined rather than transformational bets.

growth_opportunities.title: Non-OTC and US futures
growth_opportunities.description: Already past $100M and 12.6% of revenue, up from 10%. The clearest and most proven growth engine Plus500 has.

growth_opportunities.title: Prediction markets
growth_opportunities.description: First-mover clearing infrastructure through Kalshi, CME/FanDuel and Topstep in a segment expanding fast. High optionality, dependent on regulation.

growth_opportunities.title: B2B clearing and technology
growth_opportunities.description: Selling the existing stack to institutions produces sticky, high-margin, long-contract revenue. The best-quality revenue available to the group.

growth_opportunities.title: Geographic expansion
growth_opportunities.description: India through Mehta, Latin America through Colombia, plus UAE and Canadian licences. These markets are underpenetrated and their regulatory dynamics beat Europe's.

growth_opportunities.title: Further ARPU and longevity uplift
growth_opportunities.description: Proven, with ARPU up 8% and deposits doubled, though the lever is finite.

key_questions.question: Active customers fell 5% and new customers 11%. At what point does volume decline overwhelm ARPU growth, and where is the modelled floor for the OTC customer base?
What are the three-year revenue and margin targets, and what share of group revenue does non-OTC need to reach before the CFD regulatory risk is structurally de-risked?
How much capital and revenue is committed, and what happens if the CFTC regime tightens or a partner platform loses authorisation?
The 44% EBITDA margin leads the sector. What sustains it as the mix shifts toward lower-margin clearing and futures?
What is the precise revenue at risk under further European leverage-cap tightening, broken down by jurisdiction?
With about $0.8B in cash and 38% of shares already repurchased, how does the board choose between buybacks, acquisitions and organic investment?
What synergy and revenue does Mehta Equities need to deliver, and what is the integration risk?

executive_summary.overview: Plus500 runs one of the most efficient businesses in retail trading. FY2025 revenue reached $792.4 million and EBITDA $348.1 million, a 44% margin. The company carries no debt, holds about $0.8 billion in cash, and has repurchased roughly 38% of its shares over time, a capital-returns record few listed peers approach. Its proprietary technology drives both the lean cost base and a falling customer acquisition cost. The financials look excellent and the customer base is shrinking: active customers fell 5% in FY2025 and new customers fell 11%. Revenue grew only because ARPU rose 8% to a record $3,268, and because long-tenure customers now produce around half of OTC revenue.
key_findings: FY2025 revenue $792.4M, EBITDA $348.1M (44% margin), no debt, about $0.8B in cash, roughly 38% of shares repurchased over time
Active customers fell 5% and new customers fell 11% in FY2025; revenue grew only because ARPU rose 8% to a record $3,268, with long-tenure customers now producing around half of OTC revenue
Management chose value over volume and has executed that choice well, but ARPU still has a ceiling, and European regulation still caps the underlying OTC and CFD market

Non-OTC revenue (US futures, B2B clearing, prediction markets) passed $100 million and grew from around 10% to 12.6% of the group in a single year, with first-mover infrastructure positions through CME/FanDuel, Kalshi and Topstep
investment_thesis_notes: Bull case: non-OTC revenue passed $100 million and grew from around 10% to 12.6% of the group in a single year, with first-mover infrastructure positions through CME/FanDuel, Kalshi and Topstep. If that engine compounds, Plus500 stops being a mature CFD broker and becomes regulated market infrastructure, a different business carrying a different multiple.
Bear case: 87% of revenue still sits in a declining, regulatorily targeted product, and the new engine is too small and too young to offset it in time.
executive_summary.overall_rating: A high-quality asset with a mature core and a credible pivot underway.
executive_summary.recommendation: Proceed to deeper diligence on three questions. First, find the sustainable floor of the OTC customer base and the true ceiling of the ARPU lever, since the entire revenue line currently depends on the second outrunning the first. Second, test the unit economics, scalability and competitive defensibility of the non-OTC and clearing business against Interactive Brokers and the incumbents. Third, model a downside case for prediction markets under adverse CFTC outcomes. This is a well-run, cash-generative, high-quality business. What the valuation turns on is whether the pivot outruns the erosion.
